title: 21 Days of Self-Interview
subtitle: Three questions every night. A mirror that slowly develops.

phases.name: See
phases.intent: Don't rush to change anything. Just see clearly what you're actually doing, and where your time and attention truly go.

phases.name: Understand
phases.intent: Dig beneath what you saw: where do these patterns come from? What drives you — fear, longing, or a script someone else wrote?

phases.name: Choose
phases.intent: Once you see and understand, what remains is freedom: in a finite life, who you choose to become and where you spend yourself.

days.opening: You don't have to answer beautifully — only honestly. Tonight is Day 1. We start exactly where you're standing.
questions: If you had to describe your life right now honestly to a stranger, what would you say? (Not how you wish it were — how it actually is.)
Why are you willing to spend 21 nights doing this? What makes you feel "I need to see something clearly"?
If one change 21 days from now would make this feel worth it, what would that change be?
days.mirror: Record the user's raw description of their current state and their motivation for starting. This is the baseline for every later reflection.

days.opening: Time is a ledger that never lies. We don't look at how you want to spend time — we look at how you actually do.
questions: Thinking back on today, what three things did your time actually go to? Roughly what share each?
Which part of today did you actively choose, and which part swept you along until you looked up and it was gone?
If today were an ordinary day in your life — and most of your days look like this — how do you feel about that?
days.mirror: Notice the gap between the user's described time allocation and the life they said they wanted on Day 1.

days.opening: Time is finite; energy even more so. Tonight: what feeds you, and what quietly drains you.
questions: Was there a moment today when you felt lit up, fully alive? What were you doing?
Was there something that left you hollowed out afterward — even if it looked like something you "should" do?
If you could delete one thing that constantly drains you, what would it be? What's stopping you from deleting it?
days.mirror: Distinguish 'draining but cherished' from 'draining and resented' — the latter is a focus for later phases.

days.opening: You slowly become whatever you point your attention at. Tonight we look at what's been taking yours.
questions: Where did your attention drift most today? (Phone, a certain person, an unresolved thing, a worry...)
Is there something you keep calling important that you've barely given any real attention to?
If someone could see where your attention actually went today, what would they assume matters most to you? Does that match your own answer?
days.mirror: Contrast the user's stated priorities with where attention actually flows; point out the mismatch gently.

days.opening: The mind is a skilled liar; the body, less so. Tonight we listen to what your body knows but you haven't admitted yet.
questions: What state is your body in right now? Where is it tight, tired, or at ease?
Lately, has an emotion shown up in your body first — a clenched stomach, stiff shoulders, a sleepless night? In what situation?
If your body could speak, what's the one thing it would most want to tell you today?
days.mirror: The body is often the doorway to suppressed emotion. Note the somatic signals the user mentions and their contexts.

days.opening: We're different people in front of different people. That's not fakeness — it's survival. Tonight, which masks do you wear?
questions: In front of which person or situation are you least yourself? Who are you playing in that moment?
Is there any setting where you can drop the act completely and be real? If not, what does that tell you?
Which side of yourself are you most afraid others will see? If it were seen, what do you fear would happen?
days.mirror: Masks and the 'most feared exposure' often point to core shame or an unintegrated self. Hold it carefully, without judgment.

days.opening: Phase one, "See," closes tonight. No judging — we just piece together what this week revealed.
questions: Reading back over what you said these six days, which line moves you most right now?
This week, was there a fact about how you actually live that you'd never truly faced before?
If you summed up "the self I saw this week" in one sentence, what would it be?
days.mirror: First formal reflection. Quote the user's own words from the past six days; weave the fragments into a preliminary self-portrait and hand it back.

days.opening: Welcome to phase two, "Understand." So much of what we do and don't do is rooted in fear. Tonight we see its face.
questions: Is there something you've long wanted to do but keep putting off? If you honestly ask "what am I afraid of," what's the answer?
Your deepest fear — failure, abandonment, not being enough, being seen through, dying unremarkable — which one cuts closest?
When and how did this fear first enter your life? Do you remember the earliest time?
days.mirror: Identify the user's core fear archetype and try to connect it to the avoidance behaviors mentioned the previous week.

days.opening: The flip side of fear is often longing. Tonight we look at what you truly want — not what you should want, but what you really do.
questions: Setting aside every "should," what does the deepest part of you long for? Does saying it out loud embarrass you a little?
When did you last want something badly, then tell yourself "forget it, it's not realistic"? What was it?
If you were guaranteed not to fail and not to be mocked, what would you go after immediately?
days.mirror: Contrast the user's true longing with where their time/attention actually went on Days 2-4. The gap is where the tension lives.

days.opening: Much of how we live wasn't chosen — it's a script inherited from family and culture. Tonight, whose script are you performing?
questions: What "a person should..." sentences were drilled into you growing up? Which one still runs you today?
If you trace a current way of living or standard back to its source, whose voice is it really? (A parent, a teacher, a cultural expectation?)
If you could tear up one rule someone else wrote for you that you've kept obeying, which would you tear up?
days.mirror: Identify conflicts between inherited scripts and the user's real values — key groundwork for the 'Choose' phase.

days.opening: Fate is often just repetition. Tonight we find the loop that keeps replaying in your life.
questions: Is there a pattern that recurs in your life? (The same kind of relationship, the same sticking point, the same giving-up halfway...)
Each time the loop begins, what do you feel inside? How do you usually respond?
If this loop is 'protecting' you from facing something, what is it helping you avoid?
days.mirror: Tie the repeating pattern to Day 8's fear and Day 10's script, presenting a chain of cause and effect.

days.opening: Who we are develops largely in our relationships with others. Tonight, who are you when you're with people?
questions: In your closest relationships, are you more often the one who gives and pleases, or the one who takes and withdraws? Or does it depend?
When did you last truly feel understood? When did you last feel lonely?
What do people most often misunderstand about you? Is any part of that misunderstanding something you created yourself?
days.mirror: Attend to the user's attachment posture and loneliness; connect to Day 6's masks — the masks worn in relationships are often the heaviest.

days.opening: Regret isn't for wallowing — it's for reading what you truly care about. Tonight we read it.
questions: Looking back right now, what's your single biggest regret? Something you did, or something you didn't?
What is this regret telling you that you deeply cared about but didn't treat well?
If nothing changes, five years from now what choice — or non-choice — of today are you most likely to regret?
days.mirror: Regret reveals a value that wasn't honored. Note it — circle back when discussing values on Day 15.

days.opening: Phase two, "Understand," gathers tonight. We piece fear, longing, script, and repetition into a map.
questions: Digging deeper this week, what discovery about "why you are the way you are" surprised you most?
Fear, longing, inherited script, the repeating loop — which of these has the largest grip on you today?
If the first two weeks showed you what shaped you, where do you most want to start taking back the reins?
days.mirror: Second major reflection. Integrate the threads from Days 8-13 into a 'causal chain,' then transition naturally toward the Choose phase.

days.opening: Welcome to the final phase, "Choose." Choice begins with knowing what you truly value. Tonight we name it plainly.
questions: Without borrowing any grand words, in your own language: what are the three things you truly value in this life?
Looking at the regrets and longings you named these past two weeks, what one or two shared values do they point to?
How much of your current daily life is actually lived by those values? Give it a score from 0 to 10.
days.mirror: Quote Day 9's longing and Day 13's regret to help the user distill their own core values — in their words. Don't name it for them.

days.opening: Existentialism has a hard line: you are freer than you think, and therefore more responsible. Tonight we face it.
questions: Take the thing you keep telling yourself "I can't, because..." and swap "can't" for "I choose not to." What does the sentence become? How does it read?
Where in your life is there actually a choice that you keep treating as "this is just how it has to be"?
If you fully admit "my current life is the result of a series of my own choices," do you feel heavier, or a little freer?
days.mirror: Gently reframe 'forced' as 'chosen' to restore the user's sense of agency — not to manufacture guilt. Hold the emotion that surfaces.

days.opening: Tonight's questions are a little heavy, but they're the most honest wake-up: we all die, and time is finite.
questions: If you knew for certain you had five years left, what would you stop doing immediately? What would you start immediately?
Picture yourself old, sitting in a chair, looking back on this life. What one sentence do you most hope that self can say?
Knowing time is finite, what suddenly feels precious enough that you shouldn't keep postponing it?
days.mirror: Death awareness is for clarifying priorities, not generating anxiety. Align answers with Day 15's values and point to something doable now.

days.opening: Authenticity isn't the breezy "just be yourself." It means aligning the inside with the outside, even at a cost. Tonight we talk about it.
questions: What do you believe inside but have never lived out, because you were afraid?
On what specific thing is the gap between your outward behavior and your inner truth the widest? What has that gap cost you?
What's the lowest-cost first step toward being more truly yourself — small enough that you'd dare promise it to yourself tonight?
days.mirror: Bring the grand idea of 'authenticity' down to one concrete, doable step. Echo the Day 6 masks theme.

days.opening: After seeing, understanding, and thinking it through, meaning finally lands on commitment. Tonight we turn thought into a promise.
questions: Based on everything you've seen these 18 days, is there one thing you're now willing to solemnly commit to yourself — to do, or to stop?
When did you last make yourself a promise and not keep it? What's different this time that could make it actually happen?
To keep this commitment, what do you need to set down, or say no to?
days.mirror: Help the user make the commitment concrete and measurable, and anticipate resistance (echoing Day 11's repetition pattern).

days.opening: Meaning rarely closes its loop on the self alone. Tonight we look outward a little: your connection to something larger than you.
questions: Of the things you've done, which one truly helped someone, or left something behind? How does recalling it feel?
If your existence were to leave something for the world — even just for the few people around you — what would you want that to be?
Is there a kind of giving or creating during which you lose track of time, and of yourself? What might that be pointing to?
days.mirror: Self-transcendence is a major source of meaning. Note the user's 'self-forgetting moments' — they often point to gifts and calling.

days.opening: The last night. 21 days ago we started from a blank page. Tonight we look together at the self-portrait that slowly developed.
questions: After these 21 days, if you answered Day 1's question again — "what is your life like right now" — how is your answer different?
Your single most important discovery on this journey, distilled into one sentence — what is it? Write it down as a word to yourself.
Tomorrow there's no one here to ask you. How will you carry forward the honesty-with-yourself these 21 nights built?
days.mirror: The finale. Weave the key quotes from the whole journey into a complete self-portrait and hand it back: what they saw, understood, and chose. Dignified, warm, never preachy. This is the climax of the entire journey.
